description: WEC Energy Group is a Milwaukee-based regulated utility holding company providing electric and natural gas service across parts of the Midwest through subsidiaries including We Energies and Wisconsin Public Service.

overview.title: Business Profile
paragraphs: WEC Energy Group is a regulated electric and natural gas utility holding company serving customers in Wisconsin, Illinois, Michigan and Minnesota. Its operating footprint is built around familiar local utility brands rather than a single consumer-facing WEC product, which is how monopoly infrastructure usually dresses for neighborhood respectability.

For the registry, the core company surface should be treated as utility service delivery: electric distribution, natural gas delivery, generation ownership, transmission investment exposure and customer account operations.

overview.title: Primary Products And Services
paragraphs: The repo’s seed products, We Energies Electric Service and Wisconsin Public Service, map to WEC’s regulated Wisconsin utility operations. We Energies serves electric and gas customers primarily in Wisconsin, while Wisconsin Public Service serves electric and natural gas customers in northeastern and north central Wisconsin.

Additional WEC subsidiaries broaden the company’s relevance beyond electric utilities into gas distribution, storage and power generation ownership, including Peoples Gas, North Shore Gas, Michigan Gas Utilities, Minnesota Energy Resources, Upper Michigan Energy Resources, Bluewater Gas Storage and We Power.

overview.title: Registry Relevance
paragraphs: WEC belongs in the utilities sector because its public-interest impact is concentrated in essential infrastructure: household energy access, business energy costs, grid reliability, generation mix and regulated rate recovery. Customers can comparison-shop a thermostat more easily than the wires behind the wall.

Potential alternative mappings should focus on community solar, municipal utilities, public power, demand-response platforms, energy efficiency programs, distributed storage and open grid-data tools rather than conventional software substitutes.
